rt2-2-2.title: Hands-on: protect stored film from radiation fog
rt2-2-2.intro: Stored film integrates dose continuously - every stray mR builds base-plus-fog and steals contrast from the whole box. Work distance, lead shielding, and exposure-time planning so a 20 Ci Ir-192 shot leaves the film store under a 2 mrem budget.

tasks.label: Double the film store's distance to quarter the rate
tasks.hint: Inverse square: with no slabs stacked, drag the marker out to twice its start distance and watch the meter fall to one quarter - 'well away from exposure bays' is physics, not habit.

tasks.label: Stack lead until the store wall reads under 0.5 mR/h
tasks.hint: Each slab is one half-value layer (about 4.8 mm of lead for Ir-192) and halves the rate - this is the lead-lined film bin at room scale; expect around seven slabs.

tasks.label: Plan the 30-min shot under the film's 2 mrem budget
tasks.hint: Dose = rate x hours, and film fogs by accumulation - cut the rate with distance plus lead until 30 minutes of beam time costs the stock less than 2 mrem.

rt2-2-3.title: Hands-on: run a timed wet process start to finish
rt2-2-3.intro: The darkroom's five steps run on order and clock discipline - develop, stop, fix, wash, dry, full time at temperature, never by eye. Drill that discipline on a six-station wet-process line: skip a station or cut the timed dwell short and the part resets, exactly as sight development ruins a film.

tasks.label: Work all six stations in their fixed order
tasks.hint: Like develop-stop-fix-wash-dry, the order is one-way: start at pre-clean and end at inspection - a swapped or skipped station resets the part.

tasks.label: Let the timed dwell run to zero - no early pulls
tasks.hint: The time-temperature method, not your eye, ends a chemical step: wait for the timer ring to close completely before moving to the next station.

tasks.label: Inspect and click the indication a clean process earned
tasks.hint: Only a full-order, full-time process leaves a readable record - scan the plate under the UV lamp for the glowing bleedout.

rt2-2-4.title: Hands-on: hold density inside QC action limits
rt2-2-4.intro: A processor's daily control strip has one job: keep film density pinned inside action limits while conditions drift. Land density in a tight 2.35-2.65 window, then catch and correct the out-of-limits drift that 30 days of source decay causes - the same read-the-chart, fix-the-cause loop processor QC runs every morning.

tasks.label: Land density inside the 2.35-2.65 action window
tasks.hint: Treat 2.5 as the aim line and +/-0.15 as the QC chart's action limits - fine-tune exposure time until the density readout settles inside the window.

tasks.label: Catch the 30-day drift and re-center the density
tasks.hint: Activity fell to about 75% (A = A0 x 2^(-30/73.8)), so the strip now reads low - like any out-of-limits chart point, find the cause and correct: stretch the time about 1.3x.
